danger_signals.visual_form: alien creature sprites and hostile entities
danger_signals.meaning: primary threat; game world has been invaded/occupied by extraterrestrial forces

danger_signals.visual_form: green door entry points to mini-stages with boss enemies
danger_signals.meaning: high-danger zones; progression checkpoints with escalated threat level

danger_signals.visual_form: hidden weapons under destructible rocks requiring bazooka/grenades
danger_signals.meaning: resource scarcity; danger of being under-equipped in combat areas

danger_signals.visual_form: armor absorbs limited hits before depletion (no visual timer shown)
danger_signals.meaning: player vulnerability increasing; armor protects against environmental/enemy hazards

safety_signals.visual_form: location labeled 'Shop' (changed from 'Bar' in Japanese version)
safety_signals.meaning: safe zone for trading; player can acquire weapons and armor without combat pressure

safety_signals.visual_form: zeny currency pickups scattered in environment
safety_signals.meaning: economic safety; accumulating resources for future defensive/offensive capability

safety_signals.visual_form: visual items hidden under rocks or in environment
safety_signals.meaning: temporary power enhancement; improved combat capability against threats

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: General Kawasaki's military (allied faction in story context)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: military plane used for transport; structured locations suggesting military base staging

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: alien creature leader 'Zang Zip' as faction representative
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: generic alien enemy sprites; bosses as faction commanders
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: future/alien landscape (post-time-displacement setting); hostile environment indicates alien control

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: players hurtled into future by mysterious storm; landscape changed to alien-controlled futuristic setting
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: enemy behavior shifts from human soldiers to alien creatures; mission objective changes from rescue to survival/invasion defense

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: alien creature presence throughout environment; hostile NPC population replaces friendly forces
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: all encountered entities are hostile; no ally NPCs or peaceful areas except shop locations

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: zeny currency system present; shop infrastructure visible
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: players incentivized to explore for hidden weapons/currency; strategic equipment purchasing affects combat outcomes

environmental_storytelling.example: shop vs. bar localization difference: US version uses 'Shop' (commercial/neutral safe space), Japanese 'Bar' suggests different cultural framing of respite locations
green door access to boss mini-stages signals narrative progression; defeating bosses advances main story of defeating Zang Zip
weapons hidden under destructible rocks communicate need for player agency/exploration; environmental puzzles reward thorough investigation
NES version removes weapon ammo limits and introduces reusable weapon system; armor as consumable resource type signals shift from arcade scarcity to progression-based durability

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: default combat state; indicates immediate offensive capability but suggests limited range at game start
reverts to machine gun weapon; demotes player to lower-tier weaponry as death penalty
hostile encounters throughout; indicates total territorial control by Zang Zip faction
accessed via green doors; separate encounter contexts suggesting hierarchical threat levels above standard enemies

notes: Ikari Warriors II uses minimal explicit visual faction branding (colors/symbols limited in 8-bit NES format) but communicates world-state primarily through enemy type variation and location design. The temporal displacement narrative (modern soldiers sent to alien-occupied future) creates implicit visual contrast: player is anachronistic in futuristic setting, signaling vulnerability. NES version adds economic layer (zeny, shops, armor durability) absent from arcade, visually communicating progression/resource management as survival mechanics. Regional localization changes (Bar→Shop, credit removal) suggest localization of tone rather than world-state changes. Game communicates danger through enemy density, boss gate access restrictions, and armor depletion mechanics rather than explicit visual danger zones.
sources_used: tcrf (The Cutting Room Floor - Ikari Warriors II: Victory Road NES)
